evals.name: spawn-three-agents-on-bug
evals.prompt: I want to race claude, codex, and opencode on the same memory leak in src/cache.ts. Each should get its own git worktree and its own cmux workspace, with the same prompt. Then I want to be able to tell which one finishes first without staring at the screens.
evals.expected_output: Solution should: (1) use cmux-worktree new in a loop with -a flag for the agent and pass the same prompt; (2) explain that the sidebar rings + Cmd+Shift+U is how you know who's blocked or done; (3) mention cmux setup-hooks if not already installed; (4) include a cleanup step using cmux-worktree done for the losers. Should NOT recommend tmux, screen, or splits in a single workspace (which loses sidebar metadata).
expectations: Recommends cmux-worktree new in a loop (or its underlying git worktree add + cmux new-workspace decomposition) with -a flag for the agent, passing the same prompt to each
Mentions cmux setup-hooks (or hook installation) so agent rings/notifications work
Explains sidebar rings + Cmd+Shift+U (or equivalent attention-routing mechanism) is how you tell who's done/blocked without staring
Includes a cleanup/teardown step using cmux-worktree done for losing agents
Does NOT recommend tmux, screen, or splits in a single workspace as the primary mechanism (single-workspace splits lose sidebar metadata)

evals.name: ci-watcher-pings-workspace
evals.prompt: I have several feature branches each open as cmux workspaces. Write me something that polls GitHub Actions every minute and rings the cmux tab whose branch matches a failing run. I'm in a cmux terminal already.
evals.expected_output: Should: (1) use cmux list-workspaces --json and jq to find the workspace whose branch field matches the failing branch; (2) use cmux notify --workspace <ref> to fire the notification; (3) use cmux set-status with --color red as a sticky indicator; (4) recommend resolving handles to UUIDs once if the script runs long. Should NOT use index-based handles (workspace:2) in a long-running loop.
expectations: Uses cmux list-workspaces --json piped through jq to find the workspace whose branch field matches the failing branch
Uses cmux notify --workspace <ref> to fire the notification on the matching workspace
Uses cmux set-status (with --color red or similar) as a sticky/visible indicator on the failing workspace
Recommends resolving the workspace handle to a stable identifier (UUID or ref captured up front) rather than relying on a literal index like workspace:2 inside a long-running poll loop
Polls every minute (sleep 60 or equivalent) and uses gh / GitHub Actions API to detect failing runs

evals.name: explain-claude-teams-vs-omc
evals.prompt: What's the difference between cmux claude-teams and cmux omc? Which should I use if I want one Claude that spawns a few sub-agents to parallelize a refactor?
evals.expected_output: Should explain: (1) claude-teams uses Claude's experimental teammate mode and is the lighter-weight option; (2) omc is oh-my-claudecode, a separate npm package with 19 agents and pre-built pipelines; (3) for one Claude with sub-agents, claude-teams is the right answer; (4) both use the same tmux-shim trick at ~/.cmuxterm/<agent>-bin/tmux to render sub-agents as native cmux splits.
expectations: Explains claude-teams uses Claude Code's experimental teammate mode (CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1) and is the lighter-weight option
Explains omc is oh-my-claudecode, a separate / external npm package with its own pre-built multi-agent pipeline (mentions ~19 agents or similar specifics)
Recommends claude-teams as the right answer for one Claude with sub-agents on a refactor
Mentions both use the same fake tmux shim trick (e.g., ~/.cmuxterm/<agent>-bin/tmux) to render sub-agents as native cmux splits

evals.name: wire-worktree-palette-action
evals.prompt: Add a 'New worktree + claude' action to my repo's cmux.json so I can trigger it from the command palette. The script that does the work is called cmux-worktree.
evals.expected_output: Should: (1) produce a cmux.json snippet with an actions.<id> entry of type 'command'; (2) set palette: true; (3) command should be 'cmux-worktree new' (and explain that static commands can't prompt for input, so the user types the slug in the resulting terminal); (4) optionally add an icon. Should NOT invent fields not in the cmux.json schema.
expectations: Produces a valid cmux.json snippet with an actions.<id> entry of type 'command'
Sets palette: true so it appears in the command palette
command field is set to 'cmux-worktree new' (the actual binary/script name)
Notes/explains that type 'command' actions are static and cannot prompt for input — the user types the slug in the resulting terminal (or recommends a wrapper for fully-driven input)
Does NOT invent fields outside the cmux.json schema (e.g., no fictional input/prompt/args fields)

evals.name: drive-other-agent-from-script
evals.prompt: From a script running in workspace 1, I want to send a TypeScript error report to whichever workspace has the agent fixing the parser bug. The fixing agent's workspace is named 'fix-parser'. How?
evals.expected_output: Should: (1) use cmux list-workspaces --json | jq to resolve the ref by name field; (2) use cmux send --workspace <ref> -- '<text>\n' (with explicit newline) to deliver the message; (3) optionally use cmux notify alongside; (4) explain that without \n the text is typed but not submitted.
expectations: Uses cmux list-workspaces --json piped through jq (or equivalent) to resolve the workspace ref by its name field rather than hardcoding a workspace index
Uses cmux send --workspace <ref> to deliver the error-report text to the resolved workspace
Includes an explicit \n (newline) at the end of the cmux send payload to actually submit the message
Explains that without the \n the text is typed but not submitted (i.e., the newline is what triggers Enter)
Optionally suggests cmux notify alongside cmux send for attention routing
